title: Opposition
summary: The single most important endgame concept

steps.title: Face to Face
steps.text:
## What is the opposition?

**Opposition** is the situation where two kings stand on the same file, rank, or diagonal with **exactly one square between them**. The side **NOT** to move is said to *have* the opposition — the other king must give ground.

### Types you must know

- **Direct opposition** — kings one square apart on a file or rank (most common).
- **Diagonal opposition** — kings one square apart on a diagonal.
- **Distant opposition** — kings on the same line with **3 or 5** squares between them.

A simple test: if the count of squares between the kings is **even**, the side *to move* loses the opposition.

### Why it matters

In pure king-and-pawn endings, the opposition decides who can pass and who must retreat. Many drawn-looking positions are won — and many won-looking positions are drawn — purely on this single tempo.

### Common mistakes

- Stepping forward to the wrong square so the *opponent* gets the opposition.
- Confusing direct with diagonal opposition.
- Pushing a pawn while you have the opposition — you may give it back for free.

> The side that must move first, must move aside.

steps.title: With a Pawn
steps.text:
## Opposition with a pawn

Now White owns an extra **pawn on e4** and a king on **e6**. Black's king on **e8** stands in the way of the queening square. To win, White must combine **two ideas**:

1. Keep the king **in front of the pawn**, never behind it.
2. **Outflank** the enemy king by stepping diagonally to grab a key square.

### Key squares

For a pawn on the 5th rank or lower, the **key squares** are the three squares **two ranks ahead** of the pawn (here: d6, e6, f6). If your king reaches any one of them with the move, you win regardless of opposition.

### Procedure

1. Step the king diagonally forward: {{Kd6}} or {{Kf6}}.
2. The black king must give way; follow him along the file.
3. Push the pawn only when it can advance with the king's protection.
4. Promote and switch to the K+Q mate.

### Common mistakes

- Pushing {{e5}} first — Black plays Ke7 and you lose all progress.
- Going Ke6-e7?? — illegal? actually Ke7 walks into check from the king. Don't.
- Playing Ke4 / Kf4 — the king drifts away from his pawn.

### Model line

{{Kd6}} Kd8 {{e5}} Ke8 {{e6}} Kf8 {{Kd7}} and the pawn queens.

> King in front, pawn behind.

steps.prompt: Step **diagonally forward** to seize a key square. The principle: *the king leads, the pawn follows*.
steps.hint: Both flanks work — step to the **6th rank** and force Black to choose which side to retreat.
